worth_it_explanation: Buyer and seat controls decide which demand sources can bid on a publisher's inventory, so they sit directly between the inventory and the revenue it earns. Lists that grow by accretion, with blocks added during incidents and never revisited, can keep paying buyers out of the auction long after the reason for the block has passed. The check is a review of the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder configuration a publisher already maintains in their Magnite setup, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific list entry the publisher can keep, narrow, or remove on evidence.
intro: Buyer and seat controls govern which demand-side platforms, agencies, and individual buyer seats are allowed to bid on a publisher's inventory through Magnite, a sell-side platform (SSP). This subcategory is about the intent behind those lists, whether the allowlists and blocklists reflect deliberate decisions a publisher can explain, rather than entries that accumulated over time and were never reviewed. These checks confirm that buyer and seat controls are maintained on purpose and that the blocks in place are scoped tightly enough that legitimate demand still reaches the auction.

audit_checks.title: Buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder settings are not maintained intentionally
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyer and seat controls that grow by accretion, with entries added during incidents and never revisited, drift away from the publisher's current intent. Stale blocks can keep paying demand out of the auction while stale allowances can leave a buyer enabled that the publisher would no longer choose, so a list nobody reviews stops reflecting the decisions it was meant to carry.

educate.base:
A sell-side platform (SSP) such as Magnite lets a publisher decide which demand can reach their inventory. That decision is carried by lists, an allowlist of buyer seats, demand-side platforms (DSPs), and agencies that may bid, a blocklist of those that may not, and the bidder configuration that determines which demand partners are enabled at all. Each entry on one of those lists represents a choice the publisher made at some point about who should and should not have access to the auction.

The problem is that these lists tend to accumulate rather than get curated. A buyer is blocked during a brand-safety incident and the block stays in place after the incident is resolved. A DSP is added to the allowlist for a specific campaign and is never removed when the campaign ends. A bidder is disabled during a troubleshooting session and is left off because no one circles back. None of these entries is wrong on the day it is made. The issue is that a list nobody reviews stops matching the publisher's current intent, and the publisher loses the ability to explain why any given entry is there.

The audit question is not whether the lists exist. It is whether they are maintained on purpose. A publisher who can walk through the blocklist and give a current reason for each entry has a maintained list. A publisher who finds entries they cannot explain, added by people who have left or for reasons no longer recorded, has a list that has drifted.

A few signs point to buyer and seat controls that are not maintained intentionally:
  • The blocklist contains buyer seats or domains the publisher cannot give a current reason for
  • No record exists of when entries were added, by whom, or why
  • The allowlist or bidder configuration has not been reviewed on any regular schedule, only edited reactively during incidents
  • Entries added for a finished campaign or a resolved incident are still in place
You may want to confirm that each buyer and seat control reflects a decision the publisher can still stand behind. One approach is to export the current allowlists, blocklists, and enabled bidders, then walk through them entry by entry and record a current reason for each, removing or narrowing the ones that no longer hold.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, brand-safety blocks added during a news cycle or a single advertiser dispute often outlive the reason they were added. You may want to review the blocklist against the events that prompted each entry, so a block placed for a moment that has passed does not keep paying demand out of the auction indefinitely.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, buyers may have been blocked to protect a retail-partner relationship or to keep competing advertisers off product pages. You may want to confirm those blocks still match current partner agreements. A relationship that has changed can leave a block in place that no longer serves a purpose.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed documentation for buyer and seat controls is in the partner portal (login required), so it is not linkable here.

investigate.intro: Review the current buyer allowlists, blocklists, and enabled bidders in the Magnite console, and check whether a current reason exists for each entry and whether the lists are reviewed on any schedule.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Sign in to the Magnite console and open the controls that govern buyer access, typically the buyer or seat management section alongside the inventory or deal settings.
List the current allowlisted buyer seats, DSPs, and agencies, and the current blocklisted seats and advertiser domains.
For each blocklist entry, ask the publisher for the current reason it is in place, and record whether a reason exists or whether the entry is unexplained.
Note whether any record exists of when entries were added, by whom, and why, and whether the lists are reviewed on a regular schedule or only edited during incidents.

magnite_demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: If the publisher runs header bidding through Magnite Demand Manager, open the wrapper configuration and list which demand partners and bidders are enabled.
Record whether any bidder is disabled, and ask whether the disable was intentional or a leftover from troubleshooting.
Confirm the enabled-bidder set matches the partners the publisher means to work with today rather than an older configuration.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each allowlist entry, blocklist entry, and enabled or disabled bidder alongside the current reason the publisher gives for it. A setup where the publisher can give a current reason for each entry and reviews the lists on a schedule passes. A setup with unexplained blocklist entries, no record of when or why entries were added, or lists edited only reactively is a fail recorded with the specific entries the publisher cannot account for.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each buyer allowlist entry, blocklist entry, and enabled bidder with the current reason for it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder settings on {{site_name}} are not maintained intentionally, with entries the publisher cannot give a current reason for and no record of when or why they were added. Buyer and seat controls in a sell-side platform (SSP) carry the publisher's decisions about which demand may reach the auction, and a list nobody reviews drifts away from current intent, keeping paying demand out or leaving access enabled that the publisher would no longer choose. {{voice_recommend}} exporting the current allowlists, blocklists, and enabled bidders, then walking through them entry by entry to record a current reason for each and removing or narrowing the ones that no longer hold. After the review, set a regular schedule to revisit the lists so they keep matching the publisher's intent.

audit_checks.title: Buyer and category blocks are broad enough to suppress legitimate demand and fill
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block that is wider than the harm it targets removes paying demand along with the demand it meant to stop. Blocking a whole advertiser category or a broad set of buyer domains to address a narrow concern thins the auction, which lowers competition, fill rate, and clearing price across the inventory the block touches, so an overbroad block trades revenue for protection the publisher did not need at that width.

educate.base:
Blocks protect a publisher, and a block that is wider than it needs to be also costs the publisher. Every buyer seat, advertiser domain, or advertiser category that is blocked is removed from the auction for the inventory the block covers. Remove enough demand and the auction thins, competition drops, and fill rate and clearing price fall with it. Fewer bidders mean fewer chances for a high bid to surface.

The trade-off is real and it has two sides. A block placed for a genuine brand-safety or competitive reason earns its cost. A block that is broader than the concern that prompted it does not. Blocking an entire advertiser category, for example all of a sensitive vertical, to keep out a single advertiser within it suppresses every paying buyer in that category. Blocking a wide list of buyer domains to address one bad actor removes the rest of the list from the auction along with the one that mattered. The protection in each case could have been achieved at a narrower width.

Magnite, a sell-side platform (SSP), and the header-bidding tooling around it support blocking at different widths, by individual buyer seat, by advertiser domain (the badv list in OpenRTB), and by advertiser category (the bcat list using Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) category codes). The audit question is whether the blocks in place are scoped to the harm they target or whether they reach wider than they need to and take legitimate demand with them.

A few signs point to blocks that are too broad:
  • A whole advertiser category is blocked where blocking one or two specific advertisers within it would have served the purpose
  • A long buyer-domain blocklist exists where the actual concern was a small number of buyers
  • Fill rate or clearing price on a slice of inventory is noticeably lower than comparable inventory, and a broad block covers that slice
  • The reason recorded for a category-wide block names a narrower concern than the block's width
You may want to confirm that each block is no wider than the harm it addresses. One approach is to compare the stated reason for each broad block against its scope, then narrow category-wide or domain-wide blocks down to the specific seats or advertisers that prompted them, and watch fill rate and clearing price on the affected inventory after each change.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a sensitive editorial topic can prompt a publisher to block an entire advertiser category rather than the few advertisers that raised the concern. You may want to narrow such a block to the specific advertisers involved, so the rest of the category can still compete for the inventory the broad block would otherwise leave thin.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, competitor blocks can grow into wide category bans that also exclude non-competing advertisers in the same vertical. You may want to scope competitor blocks to the named competitors rather than the whole category, so product and category pages keep the demand depth that holds up clearing price.

investigate.intro: Review the width of each block against the reason recorded for it, and read fill rate and clearing price on the inventory the broad blocks cover to see whether they are thinning the auction.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Sign in to the Magnite console and open the buyer, seat, and category controls that apply blocks to the publisher's inventory.
For each block, record its width, whether it targets a single buyer seat, a set of advertiser domains, or a whole advertiser category, and the reason recorded for it.
Flag any case where the width is broader than the stated reason, for example a category-wide block placed to address one advertiser.
Note which slices of inventory each broad block covers so the reporting step can read fill and price on them.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory each broad block covers, with bid density or bid count, fill rate, and average clearing price.
Compare a slice covered by a broad block against comparable inventory without that block to see whether the block has thinned the auction.
Record any case where fill rate or clearing price is noticeably lower on blocked inventory, which points to demand the block is suppressing beyond its intended target.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the width of each block, the reason recorded for it, and the fill rate and clearing price on the inventory it covers. Blocks scoped to the specific seats or advertisers that prompted them, with no unexplained drop in fill or price on the inventory they touch, pass. A block wider than its stated reason, or a broad block that coincides with a lower fill rate or clearing price on the inventory it covers, is a fail recorded with the block, its width, and the affected inventory.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the width of each block, its stated reason, and the fill rate and clearing price on the inventory it covers above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that buyer and category blocks on {{site_name}} are broad enough to suppress legitimate demand, with at least one block scoped wider than the reason recorded for it. Every blocked seat, domain, or advertiser category is removed from the auction for the inventory the block covers, so a block wider than the harm it targets thins the auction and pulls down fill rate and clearing price along with it. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the stated reason for each broad block against its width, then narrowing category-wide or domain-wide blocks down to the specific seats or advertisers that prompted them. After each change, recheck fill rate and clearing price on the affected inventory to confirm the narrower block kept the protection while returning the demand it had been suppressing.
